questions.questionText: India is currently in which stage of demographic transition?
questions.optionB: Stage 3 (declining birth rate with already low death rate — population growth rate declining)
questions.optionC: Stage 2 (high birth rate, declining death rate)
questions.optionD: Stage 4 (low birth and death rates, stable population)
questions.explanation: Core concept: India is in late Stage 3 of demographic transition (declining fertility). Why correct: Stage 3: birth rate declining (family planning, female education, urbanization), death rate already low → population growth slowing. India's TFR (NFHS-5): 2.0 (below replacement for first time). Birth rate declining, death rate low. Why A wrong: Stage 1 = pre-industrial (both high). Why C wrong: Stage 2 = early declining death rate with still-high birth rate (population explosion — India was here in 1960-80s). Why D wrong: Stage 4 = post-transition (stable/declining population — Japan, Europe). Clinical relevance: Demographic dividend: large working-age population → economic potential if educated/employed. India's window: 2005-2055. Exam trap: TFR 2.1 = replacement level. India at 2.0 (NFHS-5) = below replacement but population still growing (demographic momentum). Memory aid: 'India Stage 3 = Birth rate Coming down, Death rate already Down.' Reference: Park – Demography.

questions.questionText: The most cost-effective and widely used method of permanent contraception for females in India is:
questions.optionB: Tubectomy/Female sterilization (minilaparotomy — most commonly performed family planning procedure in India, >80% of sterilizations)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Tubectomy is the most widely used family planning method in India. Why correct: Tubectomy (female sterilization): minilaparotomy (postpartum/interval) or laparoscopic. >36% of eligible couples use female sterilization (NFHS-5). Most common method by far. Male sterilization (vasectomy): safer, simpler, but socio-culturally underutilized in India (<0.3%). Why A wrong: Vasectomy is simpler and safer but grossly underutilized in India due to social factors. Why C wrong: IUCD — used by ~2% of women (CuT 380A: 10 years). Why D wrong: OCP — used by ~5%. Clinical relevance: India's contraceptive prevalence rate: ~67% (NFHS-5). Unmet need: ~9%. Mission Parivar Vikas: focuses on high TFR states. Exam trap: Female sterilization is most USED, but vasectomy is SAFER and SIMPLER — frequently asked comparison. Memory aid: 'India = most women get tubes tied (tubectomy dominant).' Reference: Park – Family Planning.
questions.conceptTag: Tubectomy most common sterilization India FP
